evidence_quotes.quote: Nevertheless, more and more games disappear because publishers switch off servers or block access once a title is no longer commercially attractive.
Older games can therefore continue to exist regardless of the economic interest.

paragraphs.text: Video games are an important part of our digital culture. They connect people, create communities, foster creativity and often address socially relevant issues. For many, they are more than just entertainment they are an integral part of their lives and identity. Nevertheless, more and more games disappear because publishers switch off servers or block access once a title is no longer commercially attractive. As a result, cultural works are often lost without the possibility of continuing to experience or preserve them. The Stop Killing Games initiative is working to prevent this loss. This is not a call for permanent support. The only issue is that developers provide a durable solution at the end of a game’s life cycle, for example through a final patch or the possibility to operate private servers. Older games can therefore continue to exist regardless of the economic interest. Under the Digital Fairness Act, we ask for this aspect to be taken into account. Digital content, such as video games, is a cultural good. If fairness in the digital space is taken seriously, it also includes fair handling of such content.
